imageAlt: The decoded Zimmermann Telegram as received by the German Ambassador to Mexico, January 1917, held at the US National Archives
imageCaption: The Zimmermann Telegram as received by the German Ambassador to Mexico, January 1917. National Archives and Records Administration, NARA ID 302025 / Public Domain.

longForm.title: The Telegram That Brought America In: The Zimmermann Affair, January–April 1917
longForm.excerpt: On January 16, 1917, German Foreign Secretary Arthur Zimmermann sent a coded telegram proposing a military alliance with Mexico against the United States. British codebreakers intercepted it. Thirteen weeks later, America declared war.
paragraphs: The document held at the National Archives and Records Administration as Record Group 59, Box 3905, is a single sheet of decoded cable text. At the top, in plain English, it reads: 'We intend to begin unrestricted submarine warfare on the first of February. We shall endeavour in spite of this to keep the United States of America neutral.' What follows is the paragraph that changed the war. It is not long — roughly 200 words in its decoded form. It is, in terms of its intelligence and diplomatic consequences, one of the most consequential telegrams ever transmitted.

The Zimmermann Telegram — named for German Foreign Secretary Arthur Zimmermann, who drafted and authorised it — was transmitted on 16 January 1917 via three separate routes. The primary route ran through the transatlantic cable to Washington, then to the German Ambassador Johann von Bernstorff, then onward to the German Minister in Mexico City, Heinrich von Eckardt. The telegram proposed that if the United States entered the war on the Allied side, Germany would seek a military alliance with Mexico, provide financial support, and assist Mexico in recovering 'the lost territory in Texas, New Mexico, and Arizona.' Mexico was to approach Japan about joining the alliance as well.

The telegram was intercepted by British Naval Intelligence's Room 40 — the signals intelligence unit operating out of the Admiralty's Old Building in Whitehall. Room 40 had been reading German diplomatic cable traffic since early in the war, having obtained German codebooks through a combination of chance and deliberate intelligence effort. Nigel de Grey and William Montgomery decoded the Zimmermann Telegram within days of its interception, recognising immediately that it was the most valuable piece of intelligence the unit had yet produced. The problem was using it. Publishing the telegram would reveal to the Germans that their diplomatic codes were broken. It would also require explaining how British intelligence had obtained an American diplomatic cable — since the telegram had been routed, with American permission, through the US State Department's cable line.

Director of Naval Intelligence Rear Admiral William Reginald Hall — 'Blinker' Hall, for his eye tic — managed the disclosure with considerable care. Room 40 obtained the telegram's text as it had been relayed from Washington to Mexico City through Western Union, allowing the British to claim they had intercepted Mexican commercial cable traffic rather than German diplomatic traffic. The source protection held. When Hall presented the decoded telegram to the American Ambassador Walter Hines Page on 23 February 1917, Page received it as a gift that required no uncomfortable questions.

The US State Department made the telegram public on 1 March 1917. The reaction was immediate and, in certain newspapers, theatrical. Zimmermann's decision to confirm the telegram's authenticity at a press conference on 3 March — apparently believing the admission would somehow defuse the damage — was one of the more self-defeating diplomatic acts of the war. He had calculated that Americans would be angered by British interference in the cable line and would demand to know how British intelligence had obtained State Department communications. Instead, his confirmation removed any doubt about the document's validity. American public opinion, already shifting after Germany had resumed unrestricted submarine warfare on 1 February, moved decisively against Germany.

President Woodrow Wilson had been re-elected in November 1916 partly on the slogan 'He kept us out of war.' The telegram was one of three developments — alongside unrestricted submarine warfare and the publication of the Baralong affair's reverberations — that made neutrality politically untenable by April. On 2 April 1917, Wilson asked Congress for a declaration of war against Germany. Congress voted on 6 April. The United States had been neutral for nearly three years.

The intelligence tradecraft surrounding the telegram's handling has attracted as much scholarly attention as the telegram itself. Barbara Tuchman's 1958 monograph, 'The Zimmermann Telegram,' remains the standard account of the document's origins and disclosure, though subsequent archival access to Room 40 records has allowed researchers to refine her account of the source protection mechanism. The core of her analysis — that Hall's management of the disclosure was a masterwork of intelligence diplomacy — has not been seriously contested. What has been revised is the emphasis: Tuchman was writing without access to the British signals intelligence files, which remained classified until the 1970s. The fuller picture of Room 40's capabilities, revealed in Patrick Beesly's later work, makes Hall's achievement appear larger, not smaller.

The telegram is now held in multiple archive collections. The encoded German original — the version actually transmitted — is at the British National Archives in Kew, as part of the Foreign Office records. The decoded English version, as shown to Ambassador Page, is held at NARA. A photograph of the decoded text in its telegraphic form, as received at the German legation in Mexico City, was produced by British intelligence from the Western Union files and became the public-facing document after 1 March 1917. The three versions are not identical in their details — a function of the multiple encoding and decoding stages through which the message passed. Researchers comparing them have occasionally cited discrepancies; those discrepancies are artifacts of transmission, not evidence of fabrication.

What the documentary record does not fully explain is why Zimmermann sent the telegram at all. Germany had ample reason to know that proposing a Mexican attack on American territory would, if disclosed, accelerate rather than deter American belligerence. The reasoning, as reconstructed from the surviving German Foreign Office files, appears to have been that American entry was now inevitable — Germany had already decided to resume unrestricted submarine warfare, which both sides understood would bring America in — and that creating a US-Mexico border crisis might occupy American military attention in North America and delay the deployment of American forces to France. It was a calculation that underestimated both the speed of American mobilisation and the inflammatory effect of the telegram's contents on American public opinion. As a piece of strategic logic, it had a certain coherence. As a piece of executed policy, it was a catastrophe.

sources.title: The Zimmermann Telegram (Primary Document, NARA ID 302025)

shortForm.title: Betrayed Before It Began: Operation GOLD and the Berlin Tunnel, 1954–1956
shortForm.excerpt: The CIA and MI6 spent a year tapping Soviet military communications through a tunnel under East Berlin. The Soviets knew about it from day one. A British intelligence officer had told them.
paragraphs: The CIA's internal history of Operation GOLD, partially declassified and available through the Agency's FOIA Reading Room, describes the Berlin tunnel as one of the most productive intelligence operations of the Cold War. It collected over 443,000 hours of telephone conversations and 368,000 hours of teletype traffic from Soviet military cables running under the Soviet sector of Berlin. What the internal history does not address directly — though it acknowledges the compromise — is that the Soviets knew about the tunnel before the first shovelful of earth was moved.

Operation GOLD was conceived in 1953 by CIA officer William Harvey and MI6's Peter Lunn. The plan was to dig a 500-metre tunnel from a building in the American sector of West Berlin to a point beneath Schönefelder Chaussee, where Soviet military telecommunications cables could be tapped. Planning meetings were held in London in December 1953. Among those present was George Blake, an MI6 officer who had served in Korea and was regarded as a dependable and technically capable officer. Blake was a Soviet agent. He reported the operation to his KGB handler within weeks of the meeting.

The tunnel was dug between August 1954 and February 1955. It ran 450 metres at a depth of six metres, with the tap chamber positioned directly beneath the Soviet cables. Active collection began in May 1955. For eleven months the tunnel operated without apparent interference. The KGB, informed of its existence and location before it was built, had decided not to expose it — doing so would require explaining how they knew, which would expose Blake. They let it run. On 22 April 1956, a Soviet engineering team 'discovered' the tunnel during what they described as routine cable maintenance work. The Soviets held a press conference, displayed the tunnel to journalists, and accused the Americans of criminal espionage. The Americans said nothing about the extent of the collection.

Blake was not identified as the source of the compromise until 1961, when a defector's information pointed investigators toward him. He confessed, was convicted of five counts under the Official Secrets Act, and was sentenced to forty-two years — one of the longest sentences in British legal history. He escaped from Wormwood Scrubs prison in 1966 and surfaced in Moscow, where he remained until his death in 2020. The CIA and NSA spent years assessing the damage from the GOLD operation — not from what the tunnel had collected, but from what Blake had allowed the Soviets to know about Western intelligence methods and targets. The assessment, classified for decades, concluded that the Soviets had been careful to avoid taking any action based on tunnel intelligence that would reveal their prior knowledge. The collection was real. The damage assessment was worse.

onThisDay.headline: USS Yorktown Aircraft Strike Japanese Forces at Tulagi, Opening the Battle of the Coral Sea
onThisDay.body: On 4 May 1942, aircraft from USS Yorktown (CV-5) attacked Japanese invasion forces at Tulagi in the Solomon Islands, marking the opening American strike of the Battle of the Coral Sea. The engagement that followed — 4–8 May 1942 — was the first naval battle in history fought entirely by carrier aircraft, with neither fleet's surface ships coming within sight of the other. USS Lexington was sunk and Yorktown badly damaged; Japan lost the light carrier Shoho and suffered damage to Shokaku. The Japanese invasion of Port Moresby was turned back.
